elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we introduce a novel pricing model for Uniswap V3, built upon stochastic processes and the Martingale Stopping Theorem.
elements.sent-2411-002: This model innovatively frames the valuation of positions within Uniswap V3.
elements.sent-2411-003: We further conduct a numerical analysis and examine the sensitivities through Greek risk measures to elucidate the model's implications.
elements.sent-2411-004: The results underscore the model’s significant academic contribution and its practical applicability for Uniswap liquidity providers (LPs), particularly in assessing risk exposure and guiding hedging strategies.
elements.sent-2411-005: Uniswap\cite{b1 , launched in November 2018, represents a decentralized, open-source protocol for cryptocurrency exchange.}
elements.sent-2411-006: As the first automated market maker (AMM) to achieve widespread adoption, Uniswap enables users to conduct token swaps directly from their wallets via liquidity pools.
elements.sent-2411-007: In these pools, liquidity providers (LPs) earn fees by contributing their assets.
elements.sent-2411-008: The introduction of Uniswap V3 brought significant advancements, most notably the concept of concentrated liquidity.
elements.sent-2411-009: This feature allows LPs to allocate their capital to specific price ranges, enhancing capital efficiency and yielding higher returns while simultaneously mitigating price risk.
elements.sent-2411-010: Furthermore, Uniswap v3 incorporates multiple fee tiers, thereby providing LPs with flexibility in selecting their compensation levels in accordance with their risk tolerance.
elements.sent-2411-011: With the ongoing expansion of decentralized finance (DeFi) adoption, the demands on LPs in Uniswap v3 have intensified.
elements.sent-2411-012: LPs are now required to meticulously balance a myriad of factors, including risk, returns, and volatility, necessitating a sophisticated understanding and strategic approach to liquidity provision.
elements.sent-2411-013: LVR is a metric designed to quantify the impact of adverse selection on automated market maker (AMM) LPs\cite{b2 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: LVR captures the costs incurred by LPs due to stale prices that better-informed arbitrageurs exploit.
elements.sent-2411-015: Unlike impermanent loss, which only considers the starting and ending points of a trade, LVR compares the performance of LPs to a rebalancing portfolio, highlighting additional costs associated with providing liquidity.
elements.sent-2411-016: LVR incorporates volatility into its calculations, making it a useful tool for selecting the optimal liquidity pool.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, it remains primarily a risk assessment tool.
elements.sent-2411-018: It does not evaluate the returns for LPs when exiting positions at different prices, nor does it provide guidance on the optimal range for market-making activities.
elements.sent-2411-019: Guillaume Lambert focused on pricing within the Black-Scholes-Merton (BSM) framework in his work\cite{b3}\cite{b4 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: He viewed liquidity positions as perpetual options and attempted to replicate liquidity positions with covered calls.
elements.sent-2411-021: He also extends his model towards implied volatility and hedging.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, a limitation of Lambert's model is the specification of an expiration time T, which inherently makes it an approximation rather than a precise solution.
elements.sent-2411-023: Additionally, Lambert explored the use of stochastic processes to calculate the probability of prices remaining within a position range, which has greatly inspired our research.
elements.sent-2411-024: We aim to build upon this foundation by starting from stochastic processes to initiate our study.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Martingale and first hitting problem with two boundaries
elements.sent-2411-025: Unlike traditional derivatives with specific expiration dates, Uniswap V3 contracts are perpetual, persisting until the price breaches predefined boundaries or the contract holder voluntarily ceases market-making.
elements.sent-2411-026: In scenarios where the price crosses these preset boundaries, this constitutes a classic First Passage Problem, for which Darling and Siegert provided a derivation of the stopping time distribution using the Laplace Transform in 1953\ \cite{b5 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: We have further discovered that the expected value of the payoff under the risk-neutral measure contains exclusively terms of the form $\tau^n \exp(-r\tau)$ which are associated with the stopping time $\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-028: Therefore, we employ the Laplace Transform and its implications to derive the present value when the holder does not elect to terminate prematurely.
elements.sent-2411-029: In situations where the contract holder actively ceases market-making, we draw inspiration from the pricing methodology for Perpetual American Puts described by Shreve (2004) \cite{b6 and use numerical optimization to determine the optimal stopping strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Uniswap V3 can be divided into two primary pricing components: the Liquidity Provider (LP) segment and the Rebate segment.
elements.sent-2411-031: The LP component is structured to accommodate either European or American option styles.
elements.sent-2411-032: Under the European construct, the Uniswap contract realizes its terminal value solely upon the price breaching the predetermined upper or lower thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-033: In contrast, the American framework allows the contract holder the flexibility to terminate the automated market-making process at any chosen point in time.
elements.sent-2411-034: The valuation of the Rebate segment hinges on the method selected by the contract holder for Rebate withdrawal.
elements.sent-2411-035: The upper limit scenario assumes the possibility of continuous Rebate extraction at the precise point of accrual.
elements.sent-2411-036: Meanwhile, the lower limit approach considers a singular, comprehensive withdrawal of all Rebates coinciding with the termination of the contract.
elements.sent-2411-037: We initially assume that the ETHUSDT price follows a Geometric Brownian Motion (GBM) characterized by drift $\mu$ and annualized volatility $\sigma$ : \begin{equation}dS(t)=\mu S(t)dt+ \sigma S(t)dB_t\end{equation} or alternatively: \begin{equation}S(t) = S_0\exp((\mu-\frac{\sigma^2}{2})t+\sigma B_t)\end{equation} Define the unit price of ETHUSDT as follows \begin{equation}P(t) = \frac{S(t)}{S_0}=\exp((\mu-\frac{\sigma^2}{2})t+\sigma B_t)\end{equation} We partition the pricing structure of Uniswap V3 into two segments: \begin{equation}V = V_{LP}+V_{fee}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-038: The unit price of the LP segment within the Uniswap V3 Contract, constrained by $S_H>S_0> S_L$, can be expressed as: \begin{equation} V_LP(P_t) =  L_q P(t)(\frac{1}{\sqrt{L}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{H}}), P(t)<L L_q(2\sqrt{P(t)}-\sqrt{L}-\frac{P(t)}{\sqrt{H}}), L<P(t)<H L_q(\sqrt{H}-\sqrt{L}), H<P(t)  \end{equation} where $H, L$ represent the unit bounds: \begin{equation} H = \frac{S_H}{S_0} L = \frac{S_L}{S_0} \end{equation} Additionally, the Liquidity Parameter is defined as: \begin{equation}L_q =\frac{1}{2-\sqrt{L}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{H}}}\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-039: The formulation for the rebate fee segment is comparatively straightforward: \begin{equation}V_{fee} = C_a L_q t=365\times C_d L_q t\end{equation} where $C_a, C_d$ are the daily and annual rebate rate.

elements.sent-2411-040: We are interested in evaluating: \begin{equation}V(0) = \mathbb E(V(t)) = \mathbb E(V_{LP}(t))+\mathbb E(V_{fee}(t))\end{equation} For the Uniswap V3 opsition, the stopping time $\tau$ is defined as: \begin{equation}\tau = \inf\limits_{t<\infty}\{S(t)\leq S_L  or S(t)\geq S_H\}\end{equation} If the contract cannot be terminated before the stopping time, the current valuation of Uniswap V3 can be formulated as: \begin{equation} V_{E}(0) = \mathbb E(V(t)) = \mathbb E(V_{LP}(t))+\mathbb E(V_{fee}(t)) = \mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}LP_{ H}|S(\tau)=S_H]+ \mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}LP_{ L}|S(\tau)=S_L] \mathbb +E(V_{fee}(t)) = LP_{H} \mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}|S(\tau)=S_H]+LP_{L} \mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}|S(\tau)=S_L] +\mathbb E(V_{fee}(t)) \end{equation} The normalized log-unit price process of ETHUSDT is given by: \begin{equation}\bar W(t) = \frac{\ln P(t)}{\sigma}=(\frac{\mu}{\sigma}-\frac{\sigma}{2})t + B_t\end{equation} Let us denote: \begin{equation}\mu' = \frac{\mu}{\sigma}-\frac{\sigma}{2}\end{equation} Thus, we have: \begin{equation}\bar W(t) =x +\mu't +B_t\end{equation} where $x$ is the start point, $a,b$ are designated as the unit bounds: \begin{equation} b = \frac{\ln H}{\sigma}, b' = b-x a = \frac{\ln L}{\sigma}, a' = x-a \end{equation} Moreover, it also holds that: \begin{equation}\tau = \inf\limits_{t<\infty}\{\bar W(t)\leq a  or \bar W(t)\geq b\}\end{equation} Utilizing the Laplace Transform, we can express the following expectations: \begin{equation}\mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}|S(\tau)=S_H] = \exp({\mu'b'})\frac{\sinh[a'\sqrt{\mu'^2+2r}]}{\sinh[(b-a)\sqrt{\mu'^2+2r}]}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}|S(\tau)=S_L]=\exp({-\mu'a'})\frac{\sinh[(b'\sqrt{\mu'^2+2r}]}{\sinh[(b-a)\sqrt{\mu'^2+2r}]}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation} V_{E}(0) = \mathbb E(V(t)) = \mathbb E(V_{LP}(t))+\mathbb E(V_{fee}(t)) = \mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}LP_{ H}|S(\tau)=S_H]+ \mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}LP_{ L}|S(\tau)=S_L] \mathbb +E(V_{fee}(t)) = LP_{H} \mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}|S(\tau)=S_H]+LP_{L} \mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}|S(\tau)=S_L] +\mathbb E(V_{fee}(t)) \end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-041: In Euro-like pricing, liquidity providers can only exit when the price reaches the boundary.
elements.sent-2411-042: We further propose an American-like pricing model that allows liquidity providers to exit at any time.
elements.sent-2411-043: As Uniswap V3 contracts are perpetual, the termination of the contract relies exclusively on price movements, independent of time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-044: Therefore, it can be assumed that there exist boundaries determined solely by price levels.
elements.sent-2411-045: This implies that exit strategies should be based only on price considerations, analogous to the derivation of the perpetual American put option in Shreve's work\cite{b6 .}

elements.sent-2411-067: The relationship between the volatility of the underlying asset and the pricing dynamics of the Uniswap V3 position is integral to our sensitivity analysis.
elements.sent-2411-068: Intuitively, we understand that according to the European option valuation model, the present value (PV) pricing should exhibit a monotonically negative correlation with volatility.
elements.sent-2411-069: This stems from the Automatic Market Maker (AMM) mechanism of Uniswap V3, which fundamentally behaves as a short volatility strategy.
elements.sent-2411-070: As the volatility increases, we anticipate a decrease in the valuation, signifying an inverse correlation.
elements.sent-2411-071: However, the observed orange line in Figure~\ref{fig:rebate_comparison deviates from this predicted relationship due to the dual pricing model for transaction fees, which has separate methodologies for calculating both upper and lower bounds.}

elements.sent-2411-073: Conversely, the upper bound assumes continuous trading throughout the transactional process, operating under the premise that serial transactions yield continuous fees, justifying the positive valuation associated with low volatility events.

elements.sent-2411-088: We are interested in evaluating: \begin{equation}V(0) = \mathbb E(V(t)) = \mathbb E(V_{LP}(t))+\mathbb E(V_{fee}(t))\end{equation} For the Uniswap V3 opsition, the stopping time $\tau$ is defined as: \begin{equation}\tau = \inf\limits_{t<\infty}\{S(t)\leq S_L  or S(t)\geq S_H\}\end{equation} If the contract cannot be terminated before the stopping time, the current valuation of Uniswap V3 can be formulated as: \begin{equation} V_{E}(0) = \mathbb E(V(t)) = \mathbb E(V_{LP}(t))+\mathbb E(V_{fee}(t)) = \mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}V_LP(H)|S(\tau)=S_H]+ \mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}V_LP(L)|S(\tau)=S_L] + \mathbb E(V_{fee}(t)) = V_LP(H) \mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}|S(\tau)=S_H]+V_LP(L) \mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}|S(\tau)=S_L] +\mathbb E(V_{fee}(t)) \end{equation} The normalized log-unit price process of ETHUSDT is given by: \begin{equation}\bar W(t) = \frac{\ln P(t)}{\sigma}=(\frac{\mu}{\sigma}-\frac{\sigma}{2})t + B_t\end{equation} Let us denote: \begin{equation}\mu' = \frac{\mu}{\sigma}-\frac{\sigma}{2}\end{equation} Thus, we have: \begin{equation}\bar W(t) =x +\mu't +B_t\end{equation} where $x$ is the start point, $a,b$ are designated as the unit bounds: \begin{equation} b = \frac{\ln H}{\sigma}, b' = b-x a = \frac{\ln L}{\sigma}, a' = x-a \end{equation} Moreover, it also holds that: \begin{equation}\tau = \inf\limits_{t<\infty}\{\bar W(t)\leq a  or \bar W(t)\geq b\}\end{equation} Utilizing the Laplace Transform, we can express the following expectations: \begin{equation}\mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}|S(\tau)=S_H] = \exp({\mu'b'})\frac{\sinh[a'\sqrt{\mu'^2+2r}]}{\sinh[(b-a)\sqrt{\mu'^2+2r}]}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}|S(\tau)=S_L]=\exp({-\mu'a'})\frac{\sinh[(b'\sqrt{\mu'^2+2r}]}{\sinh[(b-a)\sqrt{\mu'^2+2r}]}\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-089: For the rebate segment, if we assume that we can withdraw rebates continuously,(it is an upper bound of rebate) \begin{equation} \mathbb E(V_{fee}(t)) =E\left[\int_0^\tau C_aL_qV_{LP}(P_t) \cdot e^{-r t} d t\right] =C_aL_qV_{LP}(P_t)( \cdot E\left[\int_0^\tau e^{-r t} d t\right] =C_aL_qV_{LP}(P_t) \cdot E\left[\frac{1-e^{-r \tau}}{r}\right] =\frac{C_aL_qV_{LP}(P_t)}{r} \cdot (1-E\left[{e^{-r \tau}}\right]) \end{equation} where, \begin{equation} E [e^{-r \tau}]:=F(r) =\frac{e^{-\mu'a'} \operatorname{sh}\left(b' \sqrt{2 r+\mu'^2}\right)+e^{\mu' b'} \operatorname{sh}\left(a' \sqrt{2 r+\mu'^2}\right)}{\operatorname{sh}\left((b-a) \sqrt{2 r+\mu'^2}\right)} \end{equation} For the lower bound, we assume that we can withdraw all rebates when LPs close their position, \begin{equation}\mathbb E(V_{fee}(t)) = \mathbb E(C_aL_qV_{LP}(P_t)e^{-r\tau}\tau) = C_aL_qV_{LP}(P_t)\mathbb E(e^{-r\tau}\tau)\end{equation} where, \begin{equation} \mathbb E[e^{-r\tau}\tau]= \int_{\mathbb R}\tau e^{-r\tau}p(\tau)d\tau =\int_{0}^{\infty}\tau p(\tau)e^{-r\tau}d\tau = \mathcal L\{\tau p(\tau)\}(r) = -\frac{d F(r)}{dr} =\frac{(b-a)(e^{-a'\mu'}\operatorname{sh}[b'\sqrt{2r+\mu'^2}]+e^{b'\mu}\operatorname{sh}[a'\sqrt{2r+\mu'^2}])}{\operatorname{th}[(b-a)\sqrt{2r+\mu'^2}]\operatorname{sh}[(b-a)\sqrt{2r+\mu'^2}](\sqrt{2r+\mu'^2}) } \ \ \ \ - \frac{b' e^{-a'\mu'}\operatorname{ch}[b'\sqrt{2r+\mu'^2}]+ae^{b\mu}\operatorname{ch}[a'\sqrt{2r+\mu'^2}]}{\operatorname{sh}[(b-a)\sqrt{2r+\mu'^2}](\sqrt{2r+\mu'^2})} \end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-153: Uniswap, launched in 2018, is one of the leading decentralized exchanges (DEXs) in the cryptocurrency market, enabling users to swap tokens directly from their wallets through liquidity pools, without relying on traditional intermediaries.
elements.sent-2411-154: As the first automated market maker (AMM)\cite{b1 to achieve widespread adoption, Uniswap has played a pivotal role in the growth of decentralized finance (DeFi).}
elements.sent-2411-155: The introduction of Uniswap V3 marked a significant advancement in decentralized exchange technology, most notably through the concept of concentrated liquidity.
elements.sent-2411-156: This feature allows LPs to allocate capital to specific price ranges, improving capital efficiency and enhancing potential returns while simultaneously reducing price risk.
elements.sent-2411-157: With the ongoing growth of DeFi adoption, LPs now face a more complex landscape, balancing a range of factors such as risk, returns, and volatility.
elements.sent-2411-158: This requires a sophisticated understanding of liquidity provision strategies.
elements.sent-2411-159: In this work, we treat Uniswap V3 as an exotic perpetual derivative and use the Black-Scholes framework, along with the Martingale Stopping Theorem, to derive a closed-form pricing formula.
elements.sent-2411-160: Additionally, we perform a Monte Carlo simulation and sensitivity analysis to evaluate the model’s performance.
elements.sent-2411-161: We also compare our model with other state-of-the-art models to assess its advantages and limitations.
elements.sent-2411-162: Looking ahead, Uniswap V4 is expected to introduce even more advanced features that will further enhance the efficiency and flexibility of decentralized exchange mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-163: Our model is designed to incorporate these new features of Uniswap V4, ensuring its adaptability to future developments.
elements.sent-2411-164: Our work builds on and extends two broad strands of literature: (i) DeFi research on AMMs, and (ii) traditional financial engineering studies on market-making.
elements.sent-2411-165: Below, we discuss the most relevant works and highlight how our research contributes to these fields.
elements.sent-2411-166: A significant advancement in AMM research is the concept of Loss-Versus-Rebalancing (LVR), introduced by \cite{b2 to quantify the adverse selection costs faced by LPs in AMM.}
elements.sent-2411-167: LVR measures the performance gap between an LP's portfolio and a dynamically rebalanced portfolio, capturing the losses incurred due to stale prices exploited by arbitrageurs.
elements.sent-2411-168: Another important contribution is the work of \cite{b3 , who modeled Uniswap V3 liquidity positions as perpetual options within the Black-Scholes-Merton (BSM) framework.}
elements.sent-2411-169: Lambert's work treats LP positions as covered calls and explores implied volatility and hedging strategies.
elements.sent-2411-170: Recent studies have further advanced the understanding of AMMs.
elements.sent-2411-171: For example, \cite{b4 analyze the tradeoff between risks and returns for LPs in Uniswap V3, while \cite{b5 study the predictable losses of LPs in a continuous-time.}}
elements.sent-2411-172: Closest to our work are the models in \cite{b7 and \cite{b8 , which focus on fee revenue and use approximation techniques to obtain dynamic strategies.}}
elements.sent-2411-173: Our work is also related to the market-making literature in traditional finance.
elements.sent-2411-174: Early works include \cite{b9 and \cite{b10 , which model market-making as an inventory management problem and derive optimal bid-ask spread strategies.}}
elements.sent-2411-175: These foundational studies have been extended in many directions; see \cite{b11 and \cite{b12 for recent developments Another particularly relevant area is the study of perpetual derivatives, which share similarities with the perpetual nature of Uniswap V3 liquidity positions.}}
elements.sent-2411-176: Our work is structured into four key steps, each addressing a critical aspect of pricing Uniswap V3 liquidity positions and extending the model's applicability: Step 1: Perpetual Liquidity Position Modeling We model Uniswap V3 liquidity positions as perpetual derivatives, using the Martingale Stopping Theorem and Laplace Transform to derive closed-form pricing formulas.
elements.sent-2411-177: This approach captures the perpetual nature of liquidity positions and eliminates the need for fixed expiration times.
elements.sent-2411-178: Step 2: Monte Carlo Validation We validate our closed-form formulas through Monte Carlo simulations.
elements.sent-2411-179: The results align closely with the analytical solutions, confirming the accuracy and robustness of our model.
elements.sent-2411-180: Step 3: Greeks and Implied Volatility We conduct a comprehensive analysis of Greek risk measures (Delta, Gamma, Vega) and derive implied volatility from our model.
elements.sent-2411-181: A key comparison with the LVR framework highlights the advantages of our approach in capturing implied volatility.
elements.sent-2411-182: Step 4: Generalization to Other AMMs and Uniswap V4 We discuss the potential extension of our model to other AMMs and its adaptation to the dynamic fee structures expected in Uniswap V4.
elements.sent-2411-183: While our current focus is on Uniswap V3, we outline possible modifications to the framework to accommodate future developments in AMM design.
elements.sent-2411-184: Uniswap V3 can be divided into two primary pricing components: the LPs segment and the Rebate segment.
elements.sent-2411-185: The LPs component is structured to accommodate either European or American option styles.

elements.sent-2411-220: In this section, we apply model to calculate the implied volatility and try to generalize the model to more specific AMM case.
elements.sent-2411-221: Building upon the foundation of pricing research, we can also deduce the implied volatility of corresponding Uni V3 positions by inversely solving the pricing model.We can aggregate the implied volatility views of all Uni V3 investors at a cross-section, weighting them to observe the historical performance of the weighted implied volatility views.
elements.sent-2411-222: Notably, when we compare the historical performance of implied volatility with LVR model, we uncover some fascinating patterns.
elements.sent-2411-223: The volatility inferred by LVR is based on the assumption of no arbitrage.
elements.sent-2411-224: We possess an instantaneous LVR formula, and we also understand that the collection of fees is approximately a linear model over an extremely short period.
elements.sent-2411-225: According to the \cite{b14 , under the no-arbitrage assumption, the instantaneous LVR should equate to the instantaneous fees collected. \begin{equation} C\lambda=\frac{\lambda \sigma^2}{4} \end{equation} Based on this established equality, the sigma term within the instantaneous LVR formula can be inversely calculated by other parameters in the equation, thereby yielding an implied volatility formula based on LVR. \begin{equation} \sigma=2\sqrt{C} \end{equation} We collected historical transaction data for the ETH-USDC-005 trading pair on the Ethereum blockchain from June to December 2024 and created a comparative chart of implied volatility, the figure~\ref{fig:different_IV_histogram .}}
elements.sent-2411-226: We can observe that the historical performance of the weighted IV largely aligns with the trend of the IV inferred by LVR.
elements.sent-2411-227: Additionally, the graph presents unweighted and non-aggregated IV samples, revealing that the IV deduced by LVR predominantly resides at the upper echelon of the IV samples.
elements.sent-2411-228: This phenomenon can be attributed to our model's assumption that future returns are subject to discounting.
elements.sent-2411-229: Consequently, our model inherently presumes the advantage of early boundary attainment for profit realization.
elements.sent-2411-230: As a result, the IV derived from our model is comparatively underestimated relative to the IV inferred by LVR.
elements.sent-2411-231: While the implied volatility expressions are notably similar across the three models, key differences lie in their assumptions and specifics:.
elements.sent-2411-232: Lambert's model assumes theta approximates a Dirac delta as time approaches zero, deriving the final option price.
elements.sent-2411-233: LVR also relies on infinitesimal time.
elements.sent-2411-234: Our model uses stopping time of a stochastic process to determine expiration, providing expected maturity time.
elements.sent-2411-235: LVR and Lambert assume zero risk-free rate, equating future earnings to present value.
elements.sent-2411-236: Our model incorporates the risk-free rate for more accurate present value estimation of Uni V3 positions.
elements.sent-2411-237: LVR and Lambert can only give the instantaneous value of Uni V3 position, in hence they can't capture greeks.
elements.sent-2411-238: On the contrary, our model can easily calculate greeks to measure the risk.
elements.sent-2411-239: LVR and Lambert focus on liquidity pool pricing, ignoring range order differences.
elements.sent-2411-240: We argue range orders reflect investor views on price and volatility, making price range relevant to Uni V3 pricing.
elements.sent-2411-241: LVR and Lambert assume zero-drift geometric Brownian motion.
elements.sent-2411-242: Our risk-neutral model allows flexible drift assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-243: Like LVR, our model excels in scalability, as demonstrated in the next subsection.

elements.subsubsection-2411-008: Example of application in Uniswap V2
elements.sent-2411-245: In the case of Uniswap V2, as the liquidity bounds (LH) approach zero and infinity, the stopping time for the euro-situation tends toward infinity, rendering the model ineffective.
elements.sent-2411-246: However, the modeling approach for the amer-situation can still be applied to Uniswap V2.
elements.sent-2411-247: This is because, as the duration of the position extends, the discounted value of the collected fees has a diminishing impact on overall returns, making fee collection less attractive to investors.
elements.sent-2411-248: The price range at which investors choose to exit their Uniswap V2 positions influences the expected duration of the position.
elements.sent-2411-249: Therefore, based on the price range at which investors actively close their positions, our model can still price the Uniswap V2 position.

elements.subsubsection-2411-009: Application in Uniswap V4: Dynamic fee example
elements.sent-2411-251: Uniswap V4 has a unique mechanism called dynamic fee.
elements.sent-2411-253: Dynamic fee can be setting more flexible.
elements.sent-2411-254: One kind of dynamic fee can be adjusted in real-time based on various market conditions, like volatility.
elements.sent-2411-255: It means we can regard fee as a variable that is directly influenced by volatility.
elements.sent-2411-256: Our model can easily adapt dynamic fee mechanism, by adjusting the internal parameter C of the model into a function of volatility.
elements.sent-2411-257: We believe our model will also be highly adaptable to the pricing of Uniswap V4 positions for variety hooks.
elements.sent-2411-258: In this paper, we have constructed a risk-neutral pricing model based on the stopping time theorem to price Uniswap V3 positions.
elements.sent-2411-259: Our model capture the value of each investor's range order is predicated on the discount of all potential earnings during the period until they first exit the position.
elements.sent-2411-260: From this hypothesis, we regard the Uniswap V3 position as an option pricing problem and have derived several analytical solution based on euro-situation and amer-situation, along with the corresponding Greeks based on these pricing formulas.
elements.sent-2411-261: The Greeks can be utilized to measure the risk exposure faced by investors holding this position.
elements.sent-2411-262: We have also employed MC simulations to verify the accuracy of our formula.
elements.sent-2411-263: This pricing model successfully delineates the relationship between the value of a Uniswap V3 position, its range setting, and the market's volatility.
elements.sent-2411-264: Through this model, investors can discern the implied volatility perspective underlying their Uni V3 positions and compare it with the prevailing liquidity views in the market.
elements.sent-2411-265: Moreover, with an accurate estimation of risk exposure, investors can more readily manage their risks and steer clear of undesired exposures.
